answer: Verbs in Cupeno are agglutinative, formed by prefixing subject and/or object markers to the root (sometimes with a causative prefix 'ne-' inserted before the root), and suffixing markers for tense/aspect/person. Subject prefixes include: pi- (1pl transitive), i-/p- (1sg), e- (2sg), empu- (2pl), chi- (3sg), pem- (3pl intransitive), -n- (3pl transitive). Object prefixes include: ni- (1sg), pu- (1pl, in passive), m- (2pl), ∅ (2sg), mi-/ma- (3sg, vowel harmony), wix- (3pl). Some verbs suffix object markers for certain persons (e.g., peng- for 1pl in 'kiss us'). Causative forms insert 'ne-' before the root (e.g., 'ha' > 'neha'). Tense/aspect suffixes: -wen (past/used to for plural subjects/objects), -al (used to for 1sg or 3sg with 1pl obj), -nin (1sg past transitive), -yin (1pl past), -l (2sg past intransitive). Passives mark the patient with object prefix + modified root + -yax (2sg) or -mayax (1pl/3pl). The diagram may represent morpheme combination stages (n: nasalization/assimilation? a: applicative? c: causative, l: locative?, o: oblique?), but the core is prefix-root-suffix structure with harmony and adjustments.
